lesson.menu_desc: What Would You Do? — the second conditional to imagine and advise (If I were you, I'd...) — 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 18 — Hypothetical Situations and Giving Advice: What Would You Do?
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Vanessa Giuriati | Aula 18 — Hypothetical Situations & Advice

listenings.text: I want to tell you about the best piece of advice I ever got. A few years ago, I was stuck in a job I did not enjoy. I was afraid to leave, so I kept complaining instead of doing anything. One evening, a wise friend listened to me for a long time. Then she said something simple. If I were you, I would stop waiting for the perfect moment, because the perfect moment never comes. Imagine you are sixty, she said. Would you regret staying, or would you regret trying? That question changed everything. Suppose I had stayed in that job. I think I would feel a deep regret today. So I took her advice. I made a plan, and six months later I changed careers. Here is what I learned about giving advice. Telling someone what to do rarely works. But if you say, if I were in your situation, I would try this, it feels gentle and honest. You are not ordering them. You are imagining the world from their side. And honestly, I wish someone had told me that years earlier. A good piece of advice, given kindly, can change the whole direction of a life.

Let me tell you how I learned to give advice at work without sounding bossy. When I became a manager, I made a classic mistake. Whenever someone on my team had a problem, I just told them the answer. Do this. Do that. I thought I was being helpful, but people stopped coming to me. They felt I was not really listening. Then an older colleague pulled me aside. If I were you, he said, I would ask more and order less. So I changed my approach completely. Now, when someone is unsure, I say, if I were in your situation, I would probably start with the client, but what do you think? Suppose you tried that. What would happen? It is a small change, but it makes a huge difference. People feel respected. They own the decision. And here is the key. I always frame it as my opinion, not a command. I recommend it, but I do not force it. If I had a piece of advice for any new manager, it would be this. Imagine the problem from their side first. Offer your idea gently, and otherwise let them choose. That is how trust is built.

extra_audio.text: First, I listen carefully and imagine the problem from the other person's side. Then I offer my advice gently with the second conditional and say, if I were you, I would talk to the client first. After that, I give the reason and say, because otherwise the deadline would be at risk. Next, I soften it and make it their choice and say, but what do you think? In the end, I reassure them and say, whatever you decide, I am sure it will be the right call.
